title: Slab tray with folded rim
subtitle: A flat tray with pinched-up edges folded and reinforced at the corners
excerpt: A rectangular slab tray with its edges folded upward and reinforced at the corners with small coils. No kiln required; the finished tray holds keys, jewellery, or small dry objects. The folded-rim construction is self-supporting once the clay stiffens, no mould needed.
sourceNotes: Slab-building technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A slab tray with folded edges is built from a single
slab

cut to a rectangle. The four sides are folded upward and the corners are mitered or overlapped, then reinforced with short coils on the inside. The tray is decorative and suitable for dry objects: keys, coins, rings, small dried botanicals.
Building the tray
1. Roll and cut
Roll 150 g of clay to a 6 mm thick slab about 22 cm by 16 cm. Let it rest 5 minutes. Cut a 20 cm by 14 cm rectangle from the centre, leaving the trimmings for slurry-making and corner reinforcements.
2. Score the fold lines
Lightly score a line 25 mm in from each edge using the needle tool. These become the fold lines. Do not cut through; just score a shallow groove.
3. Fold the sides

Working on one long side at a time, fold the edge upward along the score line. Support the tray base with one hand underneath while folding. The side should stand upright at roughly 90 degrees. Do the same for the opposite long side, then the two short ends.
4. Reinforce the corners
The corners are the weak point. Use the
score-and-slip

method on the inside face of each corner. Roll four short coils about 6 mm thick and 25 mm long. Press one coil into each corner with a finger, blending it into both walls. Smooth the coil flush with the interior wall surface using the wooden rib.
Drying
Leave the tray on the board. The sides may lean outward slightly while still soft; brace them with blocks of clay or small jars placed against the outside face until the clay reaches
leather-hard
. Remove the braces once the walls hold upright independently.
Finishing
Once bone-dry, sand any rough edges and corners. Seal with PVA or paint the exterior with acrylics, then seal.

items.fix: Press paper clay slurry into the crack and seal; paper clay holds corner joints better than plain air-dry
items.cause: Corners were not reinforced with coils

items.fix: Brace the outside walls with blocks until the clay reaches leather-hard
items.cause: Clay was too soft when folded
